question_text: The incubation period (time from setting fertile eggs in the incubator to hatching) for chicken eggs is typically:
explanation: The incubation period for chicken eggs is 21 days (3 weeks). Other poultry species have different incubation periods: duck — 28 days; turkey — 28 days; goose — 30-32 days; quail — 17-18 days. During artificial incubation, eggs are kept at controlled temperature (37.5°C dry bulb), humidity (55-60% rises to 70% in last 3 days), regular turning (every 1-2 hours, stops on day 18) and ventilation. Successful hatchability of fertile eggs is 80-85% under good conditions. Modern hatcheries use forced-draft incubators with computer-controlled environment.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: 7 days is too short for any avian species' incubation; chicken needs 21 days.
elimination_hints.misconception: Drastically underestimating incubation duration.

elimination_hints.hint: 14 days is too short for chicken; this is closer to small bird species' incubation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing chicken with smaller bird species.

elimination_hints.hint: 30 days is the incubation period of geese, not chicken.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing different poultry species' incubation periods.

question_text: The optimum temperature maintained inside a forced-draft chicken egg incubator throughout the 21-day incubation period is approximately:
explanation: Forced-draft (electrically heated, fan-circulated) incubators maintain a constant temperature of 37.5°C (99.5°F) throughout the 21-day chicken egg incubation. Still-air incubators (without fans) require slightly higher temperature of 38.5-39.0°C at egg level due to temperature gradient. Critical environmental parameters: temperature 37.5°C (±0.3°C), relative humidity 55-60% for days 1-18 increased to 65-70% for last 3 days (hatching), turning every 1-2 hours up to day 18 then stopped, and continuous ventilation for fresh air supply. Incorrect temperature is the most common cause of poor hatchability.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: 30°C is too low for embryo development; embryos would die or fail to develop.
elimination_hints.misconception: Underestimating incubation temperature requirement.

elimination_hints.hint: 42°C is too high; would cause embryo death from heat stress.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing brooding temperature with incubation temperature.

elimination_hints.hint: 50°C would coagulate egg proteins and kill embryos within minutes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Massively overestimating incubation temperature.

question_text: Debeaking (beak trimming) of chicks is a routine management practice in commercial poultry farms primarily to:
explanation: Debeaking (beak trimming) is the partial removal of the upper and/or lower beak tip using an electric debeaker (heated blade) or infrared method. The main objectives are: (1) Prevent cannibalism (pecking and injuring other birds, especially around vent or wounds); (2) Prevent feather pecking which causes feather loss and skin damage; (3) Reduce feed wastage from beak-flicking; (4) Reduce egg-eating behaviour in layers. Debeaking is typically done at 7-10 days of age (precision trimming) or 8-12 weeks (re-trimming if needed). Animal welfare concerns have led to alternative methods like infrared debeaking (less painful) and management changes (lower light intensity, environmental enrichment).
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

options.text: Prevent cannibalism, feather pecking and feed wastage
Determine the sex of newly hatched chicks

elimination_hints.hint: Debeaking does not directly improve egg laying; it prevents losses from negative behaviours.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing prevention of losses with direct production improvement.

elimination_hints.hint: Vaccination uses needles/sprays/water; it has no relation to beak trimming.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing different management procedures.

elimination_hints.hint: Sex determination uses vent sexing or feather-based sexing, not beak trimming.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing debeaking with chick sexing.

question_text: Coccidiosis is a major protozoal disease of poultry causing significant economic losses. The causative organism belongs to the genus:
explanation: Coccidiosis in poultry is caused by protozoan parasites of the genus Eimeria. Multiple species infect chicken: E. tenella (caecal coccidiosis — most pathogenic, blood in droppings), E. necatrix, E. acervulina, E. maxima, E. brunetti, E. mitis, E. praecox. The parasites invade and destroy intestinal epithelial cells. Symptoms: bloody diarrhoea, weight loss, ruffled feathers, mortality in young birds (2-8 weeks). Diagnosis: faecal oocyst count, post-mortem lesions. Prevention: anticoccidial drugs (amprolium, salinomycin) in feed, live vaccines, good litter management. Coccidiosis is most common in deep litter systems where birds contact contaminated litter.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: Salmonella is a bacterial genus causing pullorum/typhoid in poultry, not coccidiosis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing bacterial and protozoal poultry pathogens.

elimination_hints.hint: E. coli is a bacterium causing colibacillosis, not coccidiosis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing different bacterial diseases.

elimination_hints.hint: Aspergillus is a fungal genus causing aspergillosis (brooder pneumonia), not coccidiosis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing fungal and protozoal poultry diseases.

question_text: The Marek's disease vaccine, one of the earliest cancer vaccines ever developed for any species, is administered to chicks at what age?
explanation: The Marek's Disease vaccine is given on the day of hatch (Day 1, in the hatchery) before chicks are exposed to the disease environment. This is because Marek's Disease Virus (MDV, a herpesvirus) causes immune-mediated tumours and the vaccine works prophylactically. The vaccine (HVT — Herpesvirus of Turkey, or live attenuated MDV) is administered subcutaneously in the neck or by in-ovo injection at day 18 of incubation in modern hatcheries. It was the first commercial cancer vaccine for any species (1970), preventing lymphoid tumours in vital organs. Marek's vaccine is essential for layers and breeders; less critical for short-lived broilers.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: Week 6 is too late; chicks need protection from day 1 before viral exposure occurs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Underestimating the urgency of Marek's vaccination timing.

elimination_hints.hint: Week 12 is far too late; many chicks would already be infected by then.
elimination_hints.misconception: Drastically delaying necessary early vaccination.

elimination_hints.hint: Marek's has no treatment once symptoms appear; vaccination must be preventive.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing prophylactic vaccination with treatment.

question_text: The starter feed for broiler chicks (0-2 weeks) typically contains higher crude protein content compared to grower/finisher feeds. The recommended crude protein percentage in broiler starter is:
explanation: Broiler starter feed contains ~22-23% crude protein to support rapid muscle development in chicks. The protein requirement decreases as birds grow: Pre-starter (0-7 days) — 22-23% CP; Starter (8-21 days) — 21% CP; Grower (22-35 days) — 19% CP; Finisher (35-42 days) — 18% CP. Energy density (ME) is also high (~3000 kcal/kg) using maize/sorghum as energy sources. Soybean meal is the primary protein source. Critical amino acids — lysine (1.2% in starter), methionine (0.5%) — must be present at adequate levels. Layer mash for laying hens contains ~16-17% CP with high calcium (3.5-4%) for eggshell.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: 10-12% protein is too low; would severely retard chick growth.
elimination_hints.misconception: Drastically underestimating starter protein need.

elimination_hints.hint: 15-16% corresponds to layer feed CP, not starter; chicks need higher protein.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing layer and starter feed protein levels.

elimination_hints.hint: 30-32% is too high; excess protein is wasted (excreted as uric acid) and uneconomical.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating protein requirement.

question_text: The Haugh unit, an internationally accepted measure of internal egg quality used in commercial grading, primarily measures:
explanation: Haugh unit (developed by Raymond Haugh in 1937) measures the height of thick albumen (egg white) of a freshly broken egg in relation to its weight, using the formula: HU = 100 × log (h - 1.7w^0.37 + 7.6), where h is albumen height in mm and w is egg weight in grams. Higher HU = better quality. Fresh eggs typically have HU 75-100 (excellent quality 'AA' grade ≥ 72; A grade 60-72; B grade < 60). HU declines with egg age and storage temperature as albumen thins due to CO₂ loss. Cold storage (0-7°C) and oil-coating preserve quality. Haugh unit is the gold standard for internal egg quality assessment in research and commerce.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

options.text: Internal egg quality based on albumen height and egg weight

elimination_hints.hint: Egg weight alone is measured by simple weighing scales, not by Haugh unit calculation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing egg weight measurement with quality assessment.

elimination_hints.hint: Shell thickness is measured separately using micrometers, not by Haugh unit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing shell quality with internal quality.

elimination_hints.hint: Yolk colour is measured using Roche colour fan (1-15 scale), not Haugh unit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing yolk colour grading with albumen quality.

question_text: Infectious Bursal Disease (Gumboro disease) primarily affects which immune system organ in young chicks, leading to immunosuppression?
explanation: Infectious Bursal Disease (IBD or Gumboro, named after Gumboro town in Delaware, USA where first identified) is caused by IBDV (Birnavirus), specifically targeting the Bursa of Fabricius — the primary lymphoid organ for B-cell development in birds (located near the cloaca). The virus destroys B-cell precursors in the bursa, causing immunosuppression that lasts the bird's lifetime. Affected chicks (3-6 weeks most susceptible) show ruffled feathers, depression, dehydration, white watery diarrhoea, and 20-30% mortality. Even survivors fail to mount immune response to other vaccinations. Prevention: vaccination at 14 days (live), 7-14 days (intermediate strains), or in-ovo. Maternal antibodies provide passive protection in early weeks.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: Spleen is a secondary lymphoid organ; IBD specifically targets the bursa, the primary B-cell organ.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing primary and secondary lymphoid organs.

elimination_hints.hint: Thymus is for T-cell development, not B-cells; IBD specifically affects B-cell precursors in bursa.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping T-cell and B-cell organs.

elimination_hints.hint: Bone marrow is involved in initial cell production but not the specific target of IBDV.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing general haematopoietic site with specific viral target.

question_text: The optimal floor space requirement per bird in deep litter system for adult layer chickens is approximately:
explanation: Recommended floor space for adult chickens in deep litter system: Layers — 0.1 m² (1 sq ft) per bird, equivalent to ~10 birds per m²; Broilers — 0.07-0.09 m² (0.75-1.0 sq ft) per bird, equivalent to ~12-15 birds per m². For chicks: 0-4 weeks 0.05 m², 4-8 weeks 0.07 m², 8-20 weeks 0.09 m². Overcrowding leads to stress, cannibalism, poor growth, increased mortality and disease spread. In cage system, layer cage size is typically 30 × 45 cm housing 4 birds (~340 cm²/bird). EU welfare standards mandate higher space (550-750 cm² in enriched cages). Stocking density is one of the most critical management parameters.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: 0.02 m² is severely overcrowded; would cause major welfare and mortality issues.
elimination_hints.misconception: Drastically underestimating bird space requirement.

elimination_hints.hint: 0.5 m² is excessive for deep litter and economically unviable for commercial farming.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating space requirement, confusing with free-range.

elimination_hints.hint: 1.0 m² per bird is suitable for free-range pasture, not deep litter housing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing free-range with deep litter space requirements.

question_text: Poultry manure is considered a valuable organic fertiliser. Among common animal manures, poultry manure is particularly rich in:
explanation: Poultry manure (combined droppings + urine + litter) is the richest farmyard manure in plant nutrients, particularly nitrogen and phosphorus. Approximate composition (dry basis): N — 3.0-4.5%, P₂O₅ — 2.0-3.5%, K₂O — 1.5-2.5%. This is much higher than cattle manure (N: 0.5%, P: 0.2%, K: 0.5%) or pig manure. The high phosphorus content is especially notable because birds excrete uric acid (containing N and P) along with faeces. Poultry manure is also rich in calcium (from layer feed) and microbial biomass. It must be composted (decomposes for 2-3 weeks) before use to avoid 'burning' crops with ammonia. Used in horticulture, vegetable production and as fish/biogas feed.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

options.text: Nitrogen and phosphorus, with significant potassium and calcium
Heavy metals like lead and cadmium

elimination_hints.hint: Carbon and silica are present but the standout nutrients are N and P.
elimination_hints.misconception: Underestimating the nutrient richness.

elimination_hints.hint: Sulphur and chlorine are minor components; the key nutrients are N and P.
elimination_hints.misconception: Focusing on minor elements rather than major nutrients.

elimination_hints.hint: Quality poultry manure should not contain heavy metals (those are contaminants from poor diets).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing nutrient content with contaminants.

question_text: Match the major poultry diseases in Column A with their causative agents in Column B and select the correct option from the choices given below.
explanation: Each major poultry disease is caused by specific pathogens: Newcastle disease (Ranikhet) — caused by Newcastle Disease Virus (Paramyxovirus); Fowl pox — caused by Fowl Pox Virus (Avipoxvirus, a DNA virus, with cutaneous and diphtheritic forms); Coccidiosis — caused by Eimeria species (protozoan parasite); Pullorum disease — caused by Salmonella pullorum (bacterium causing white diarrhoea in chicks). Each disease has specific clinical features, diagnosis, prevention (vaccination/sanitation) and economic impact requiring different management approaches.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: Newcastle is caused by NDV (Paramyxovirus), not by Eimeria (which causes coccidiosis).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing viral and protozoal causes.

elimination_hints.hint: Newcastle is viral; Salmonella causes Pullorum, not Newcastle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping viral and bacterial causes.

elimination_hints.hint: Newcastle is caused by NDV; Salmonella causes Pullorum disease.
elimination_hints.misconception: Randomly assigning pathogens to diseases.

question_text: Match the parts of the hen's reproductive tract in Column A with their primary function in egg formation in Column B and select the correct option.
explanation: Each part of the hen's reproductive tract has a specific function in egg formation: Infundibulum (funnel) — receives the released yolk from the ovary, site of fertilisation by sperm if mating occurred; Magnum (longest part of oviduct) — secretes thick albumen layers around the yolk over ~3 hours; Isthmus (~75 minutes) — adds the inner and outer shell membranes; Uterus/shell gland (~20 hours, longest stage) — deposits the calcium carbonate shell and pigments. Total transit time ~24-26 hours. The egg is then expelled through the vagina-cloaca during oviposition (laying).
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

column_b.text: Adds inner and outer shell membranes

elimination_hints.hint: Infundibulum is the funnel receiving yolk, not the membrane-adding part (which is isthmus).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing infundibulum and isthmus functions.

elimination_hints.hint: Infundibulum receives yolk; shell deposition is in the uterus (shell gland).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing infundibulum and uterus functions.

elimination_hints.hint: Infundibulum receives yolk; albumen secretion is in the magnum.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping infundibulum and magnum functions.

question_text: Match the poultry feed types in Column A with their target bird category and crude protein content in Column B and select the correct option.
explanation: Different bird categories require differently formulated feeds: Pre-starter / Starter — for chicks 0-2 weeks (broilers) or 0-8 weeks (layers), highest protein content (22-23% CP); Grower — for broilers 3-5 weeks or pullets 8-18 weeks, intermediate protein (19-20% CP); Layer mash — for laying hens (>18 weeks), moderate protein (16-17% CP) with high calcium (3.5-4%) for eggshell; Broiler finisher — for last 1-2 weeks before market (35-42 days), lower protein (18% CP) for cost economy. Each feed is precisely formulated for the bird's nutritional needs at that life stage.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: Broiler starter has highest protein (22-23%) for young chicks, not the lowest layer-grade protein.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing starter and layer feed protein contents.

elimination_hints.hint: Broiler starter is for young broilers, not for older pullets.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing starter and grower feeds.

elimination_hints.hint: Broiler starter is for 0-2 weeks, not finishing stage at 35-42 days.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing starter and finisher feeds.

question_text: Read the following Assertion (A) and Reason (R) carefully and select the most appropriate option from the choices given below.
assertion: Layer hens require feed with significantly higher calcium content (3.5-4%) than broiler feed (~1%).
reason: An average layer hen produces approximately 280-300 eggs per year, each requiring about 2 grams of calcium for shell formation, leading to high daily calcium demand.
explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Each eggshell contains ~2 g of calcium (as calcium carbonate), and a hen producing 280-300 eggs/year mobilises ~560-600 g calcium annually for shell synthesis — about 4-5% of her body weight per year. To meet this high demand, layer feed contains 3.5-4% calcium (mostly from limestone or oyster shell), much higher than broiler feed (~1%). Calcium deficiency causes thin, soft shells, low hatchability, and skeletal weakness (osteomalacia). Calcium supplementation timing matters — feeding limestone in evening when shell formation peaks improves shell quality. Vitamin D₃ is also essential for calcium absorption.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
A is true, but R is false.
A is false, but R is true.

elimination_hints.hint: The high calcium output through eggshells precisely explains why layers need more calcium in feed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to link eggshell output with feed calcium need.

elimination_hints.hint: The 2g calcium per eggshell and 280-300 eggs/year are well-documented poultry nutrition figures.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting standard poultry nutrition data.

elimination_hints.hint: Higher calcium in layer feed (3.5-4%) vs broiler feed (~1%) is a fundamental nutrition principle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the established calcium difference between feed types.

question_text: Read the following Assertion (A) and Reason (R) carefully and select the most appropriate option from the choices given below.
assertion: Egg quality (Haugh unit) declines progressively with storage time and storage temperature.
reason: During storage, carbon dioxide diffuses out through eggshell pores, raising albumen pH and causing thinning of thick albumen, reducing albumen height.
explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Freshly laid eggs have albumen pH ~7.6 (slightly alkaline) which rises to 9.0-9.7 during storage as CO₂ diffuses out through the porous shell (~7,000-17,000 pores). The high pH causes the protein-water complex of thick albumen (ovomucin) to break down, making the white watery and reducing albumen height — and hence Haugh unit. The rate of decline depends on temperature: room temperature (25°C) loses 1 HU per day; refrigeration (4°C) loses 0.3 HU per day. Cold storage (0-7°C), oil-coating (sealing pores) and modified atmosphere preserve quality longer. Quality grades — AA (>72 HU), A (60-72), B (<60) — decline accordingly.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
A is true, but R is false.
A is false, but R is true.

elimination_hints.hint: CO₂ loss and pH rise causing albumen thinning is precisely the mechanism behind quality decline.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to link CO₂ loss with albumen thinning.

elimination_hints.hint: The CO₂ diffusion mechanism through eggshell pores is well-documented egg science.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the established physics/chemistry of egg ageing.

elimination_hints.hint: The decline of egg quality with storage time and temperature is universally observed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the well-known quality decline pattern.

question_text: Read the following Assertion (A) and Reason (R) carefully and select the most appropriate option from the choices given below.
assertion: Avian Influenza (Bird Flu) outbreaks in poultry are considered a serious public health concern, not just an economic concern for farmers.
reason: Some Avian Influenza Virus strains, particularly Highly Pathogenic H5N1, can infect humans through close contact with infected birds, with high human mortality rate and pandemic potential.
explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Avian Influenza Viruses (AIVs) are Type A influenza viruses with two surface proteins — haemagglutinin (H1-H18) and neuraminidase (N1-N11). Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) strains like H5N1 cause 100% mortality in poultry within days. Although primarily a bird disease, H5N1 has crossed species barrier infecting humans (~860 cases since 2003) with ~50% case fatality rate (much higher than seasonal flu). The 1918 Spanish flu pandemic (50 million deaths) and 2009 H1N1 swine flu pandemic originated from animal influenza. Mass culling of infected flocks, biosecurity, and human protective gear are critical control measures. WHO and OIE jointly monitor AIV.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
A is true, but R is false.
A is false, but R is true.

elimination_hints.hint: The zoonotic transmission of H5N1 with high human mortality precisely explains the public health concern.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to link zoonotic risk with public health concern.

elimination_hints.hint: H5N1 zoonotic transmission with ~50% case fatality is documented by WHO.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the well-established zoonotic transmission of H5N1.

elimination_hints.hint: The public health threat from avian influenza outbreaks is a well-recognised global concern.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the established public health significance.

question_text: Arrange the following management activities in artificial incubation of chicken eggs in their correct chronological sequence from setting to hatching.
explanation: Artificial incubation follows a structured sequence: (1) Setting — placing fertile, clean, properly sized eggs (large end up) in incubator at 37.5°C, 55-60% RH; (2) Turning — rotating eggs every 1-2 hours by 90° to prevent embryonic membrane adhesion (continues until day 18); (3) Candling — examining eggs against bright light at 7-10 days to detect infertile eggs and dead embryos for removal (improves hatchability); (4) Transfer to hatcher — at day 18, eggs moved to separate hatcher unit, turning stops, humidity raised to 65-70% for hatching during days 19-21. Hatching window typically lasts 24 hours.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

items.text: Transfer to hatcher (day 18); humidity raised to 65-70%
Setting fertile eggs in incubator at 37.5°C, 55-60% RH
Candling at 7-10 days to remove infertile/dead eggs
Turning eggs every 1-2 hours throughout days 1-18

elimination_hints.hint: Transfer to hatcher (day 18) cannot be the first step; setting is the first step.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the incubation sequence.

elimination_hints.hint: Candling cannot be done before eggs are set in the incubator.
elimination_hints.misconception: Skipping the initial setting step.

elimination_hints.hint: Turning cannot precede setting; eggs must be in the incubator before they can be turned.
elimination_hints.misconception: Placing turning before setting.

question_text: Arrange the following critical biosecurity steps that must be followed when entering a commercial poultry farm to minimise disease introduction in their correct sequence.
explanation: Commercial poultry biosecurity follows a strict sequence: (1) Stop at the gate, register entry purpose, justify need for visit, declare last poultry contact (>72 hours ideally); (2) Park vehicle at the disinfection point, drive through tyre dip or spray with disinfectant; (3) Change to farm-provided clothing and footwear in the changing room (don't wear personal clothes inside); (4) Walk through the foot dip (containing disinfectant like potassium permanganate or quaternary ammonium) before entering the bird-housing area. This 'visitor protocol' breaks the chain of disease introduction. Farm staff also follow shower-in / shower-out for high-biosecurity facilities.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

items.text: Walk through foot dip into the bird housing area
Stop at gate, register entry, declare last poultry contact
Change into farm-provided clothing and footwear
Park vehicle and pass through tyre dip / spray

elimination_hints.hint: Foot dip cannot be the first step; gate registration must come first.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the biosecurity sequence.

elimination_hints.hint: Clothing change cannot precede registration at the gate.
elimination_hints.misconception: Skipping the gate registration step.

elimination_hints.hint: Tyre dip cannot precede gate registration; entry control comes first.
elimination_hints.misconception: Placing vehicle disinfection before entry registration.

question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: The Ranikhet (Newcastle) disease vaccine, an essential preventive measure for Indian poultry, includes the F-strain (lentogenic) for primary vaccination and R2B for booster vaccination.
explanation: The statement is true. Newcastle Disease (Ranikhet) vaccination follows a standard schedule in India: F-strain (lentogenic, mild) is used for primary vaccination at day 5-7 of age, given via eye drop or drinking water — provides initial immunity safely. LaSota (also lentogenic) is sometimes used as alternative or for booster around 28 days. R2B (mesogenic, moderate strain) is used as the booster vaccine at 8-10 weeks of age and again at 6 months for adult birds, providing strong long-lasting immunity. R2B is given by intramuscular injection. Repeat boosters every 4-6 months are recommended for layer flocks. This protocol controls a disease that can cause 100% mortality in unvaccinated birds.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: The F-strain primary and R2B booster protocol is the standard Indian Ranikhet vaccination schedule.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the well-established Indian vaccination protocol.

elimination_hints.hint: The Ranikhet vaccination schedule is well documented in poultry health manuals.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating well-defined vaccination protocols as uncertain.

elimination_hints.hint: Both elements (F-strain primary and R2B booster) are fully correct in the statement.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating fully correct vaccination details as partially correct.

question_text:
Study the following table comparing major poultry diseases in India by their cause, key symptoms and prevention methods, and answer the question that follows.

| Disease | Causative Agent | Key Symptoms | Prevention/Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Newcastle (Ranikhet) | NDV (Paramyxovirus) | Twisted neck, gasping, 100% mortality | F-strain + R2B vaccine |
| Marek's Disease | MDV (Herpesvirus) | Lymphoid tumours, paralysis | HVT vaccine at day 1 |
| Gumboro (IBD) | IBDV (Birnavirus) | Bursa damage, immunosuppression | Vaccine at 14 days |
| Coccidiosis | Eimeria (protozoa) | Bloody diarrhoea | Anticoccidial drugs in feed |
| Fowl pox | Fowl Pox Virus | Skin/diphtheritic lesions | Wing-web vaccination |

Based on the table, which disease is most critical to vaccinate against on the very day of hatching to prevent immune-mediated tumour development?
explanation: Marek's disease must be vaccinated against on the very day of hatching because: (1) The disease is highly infectious — chicks can become exposed within hours of placement in environment containing dust/dander from infected birds; (2) Once infected, no treatment is possible; (3) The vaccine (HVT — Herpesvirus of Turkey) needs time (7-14 days) to develop full immunity before viral challenge occurs; (4) Marek's causes lymphoid tumours in vital organs, paralysis and death weeks/months later. Newcastle and Gumboro vaccines are given later (5-7 days and 14 days respectively) because of maternal antibody interference. Marek's vaccination at day 1 (in the hatchery, often subcutaneously in the neck or in-ovo at day 18 of incubation) is the only effective preventive measure.
topic: Poultry Farming: Breeds and Production Systems
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: Newcastle is vaccinated at 5-7 days, not day 1; it does not cause tumours.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Newcastle and Marek's vaccination timing.

elimination_hints.hint: Gumboro is vaccinated at 14 days and causes immunosuppression, not tumours.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Gumboro symptoms with Marek's tumours.

elimination_hints.hint: Coccidiosis is a protozoal disease prevented by drugs in feed, not by vaccination on day 1.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing protozoal disease with viral tumour disease.
